title_en: How China’s ‘Crystal Capital’ Cornered the Market on a Western Obsession
title_zh: 中国的“水晶之都”如何在西方热潮中稳占上风
rubric_en: Decades ago, Donghai was a backwater county. Today, thanks to an army of 24/7 livestreamers, it orchestrates a multibillion-dollar global industry.
rubric_zh: 几十年前，东海只是个不起眼的偏远县区。如今，依靠一支全天候直播团队，它已经组织起一个价值数十亿美元的全球产业。

paragraphs.en: At the Big Purple Crystal warehouse in eastern China’s Donghai County, hundreds of imported amethyst geodes are lined up in rows, their spiky insides open as if mid-scream. Some are shaped like eggs and perched on carved wooden stands. One crystal rides on the back of a bixi, a mythical creature with the body of a turtle and the head of a dragon. On shelves and in boxes lining the floor are countless smaller geodes roughly the size of fists, the kind you might find in a new age gift shop. The owner, Liu Junwen, sits in flip-flops behind his desk, which is, of course, an enormous amethyst geode topped with glass.
paragraphs.zh: 在中国东部东海县的一家“大紫水晶仓库”里，成排摆放着数百颗进口紫水晶石笋，尖尖的内壁像被撕开了一般，仿佛正要发出一声尖叫。它们中有的像鸡蛋一样，放在雕花木架上。还有一颗水晶骑在“貔貅（bixi）”的背上——这种神兽有着乌龟的身体和龙的头。沿着地板的架子和盒子里，散落着无数更小的石笋，大约和拳头差不多大，像你在新纪元商店里可能见到的那种。店主刘俊文穿着拖鞋坐在办公桌后面，而办公桌的桌面当然也是一颗巨大的紫水晶石笋，外面罩了一层玻璃。

paragraphs.en: Here are 23 ways China is rewiring the future.
paragraphs.zh: 以下是中国正在重塑未来的 23 种方式。

paragraphs.en: Mineral abundance isn’t unusual in Donghai. Liu may not even be the biggest operator on this block. Over the past few decades, the county has transformed from an unremarkable backwater into the “crystal capital” of China and, arguably, the world. In 2023, its crystal trade was estimated to be worth more than $5.5 billion. Liu is among some 300,000 people—about a quarter of local residents—who work directly in the business as dealers, wholesalers, livestreamers, gemologists, crystal cutters, factory managers, bead stringers, exporters, and freight agents. Together, they orchestrate a global market: The tower of Brazilian amethyst in a London yoga studio, the Colombian quartz on the reception desk of a Miami Botox clinic, the Zambian citrine in an overpriced tourist shop in Tulum—chances are, a lot of them passed through Donghai.
paragraphs.zh: 东海的矿产富集并不罕见。刘可能甚至不是这一条街上最大的经营者。过去几十年里，这个县从一个不起眼的边远县份，转变成中国乃至或许是全球的“水晶之都”。据估计，2023 年该地的水晶贸易规模超过 55 亿美元。刘是约30万人的一员，这大约占当地居民的四分之一，这些人直接从事这项业务，身份包括经销商、批发商、主播、宝石鉴定师、水晶切割师、工厂经理、串珠工、出口商和货运代理。凭借他们的协作，他们构建起了一个全球市场：伦敦一家瑜伽馆里的巴西紫水晶塔、迈阿密一家肉毒素诊所接待台上的哥伦比亚石英、图卢姆一家高价游客商店里的赞比亚黄水晶——这些很可能都曾经过东海。

paragraphs.en: Liu is the first to admit that none of this is normal. “I grew up in a village where almost every household was impoverished,” he says. “It feels unbelievable that crystals could make you rich.” But stories like his are common in Donghai. Nearly everyone we spoke with, from cab drivers and young entrepreneurs to livestreamers and longtime traders, described it as a place defined by surges of opportunity followed by sudden crashes, where information travels fast and margins can evaporate overnight. People are constantly rushing to buy and sell the shiniest new thing, often only to realize the market has already shifted and someone else caught the wave first.
paragraphs.zh: 刘是第一个承认这一切都不“正常”的人。“我在一个几乎每户都很贫穷的村庄长大，”他说。“水晶竟然能让你发财，这种事真是难以置信。”但像他这样的故事在东海很常见。我们采访的几乎每个人——从出租车司机和年轻创业者，到主播和做了多年的老交易员——都把这里形容为一个先是机会浪潮一波接一波，继而又突然而至暴跌的地方，消息传得很快，利润也可能一夜之间蒸发。人们总是在拼命抢着买进和抛出最亮眼的新东西，却常常等到买进时才发现，市场已经换了手，别人在最先的浪头上站稳了。

paragraphs.en: Western lawmakers and analysts have long argued that China became the world’s factory by exploiting cheap labor and distorting global markets. Donghai offers an alternative account: It shows how China’s industrial rise emerged in large part from rural villages, at a moment when hundreds of millions of people were desperate to escape poverty and saw global capitalism as a way out. The county became a glittering crystal mecca not necessarily because of top-down industrial policy, but because of a nearly all-consuming drive to hustle.
paragraphs.zh: 西方立法者和分析人士长期以来一直认为，中国之所以成为世界工厂，是因为它利用了廉价劳动力并扭曲了全球市场。东海提供了另一种叙事：它表明，中国的工业崛起在很大程度上是从农村乡村中冒出来的，当时数亿人都在拼命想摆脱贫困，并把全球资本主义视为摆脱困境的出路。这个县之所以成为一个耀眼的水晶圣地，不一定是因为自上而下的工业政策，而是因为一种几乎全身心投入、近乎不知疲倦的“拼命干”劲头。

paragraphs.en: “Nature has been very kind to Donghai,” explains a plaque at the Donghai Crystal Museum, a more than 300,000-square-foot building shaped like a giant jagged crystal. The county sits on the Tan-Lu fault, which stretches along China’s eastern coast. Over millions of years, tectonic activity created fractures that filled with silica-rich fluids, which slowly crystallized into deposits of clear quartz.
paragraphs.zh: “大自然对东海非常仁慈，”东海水晶博物馆的一块展牌这样解释：该馆建筑面积超过30万平方英尺，外形像一块巨大的锯齿状水晶。东海位于沿中国东海岸延伸的汤鲁断裂带之上。经过数百万年，构造运动形成了裂隙，这些裂隙中充满了富含二氧化硅的流体，最终缓慢结晶，生成了清澈的石英矿床。

paragraphs.en: For generations, farmers in Donghai found crystals in their fields and turned them into jewelry and ornaments. After the Communists took over in 1949, crystal mining was folded into the planned economy, and commercial extraction was banned. The central government recognized quartz as a strategic natural resource, useful for things like making optical lenses, and over time a sizable civilian workforce sprouted up in Donghai. When Mao Zedong died in 1976, the father of the Chinese Communist Party was laid to rest in a transparent coffin made with the finest Donghai quartz.
paragraphs.zh: 几代以来，东海的农民在田间就能找到水晶，并将其制作成珠宝和装饰品。1949年共产主义者接管后，水晶开采被并入计划经济体系，商业性开采被禁止。中央政府将石英视为一种战略性自然资源，可用于制造光学透镜等用途，随着时间推移，东海也逐步形成了可观的民用劳动力队伍。1976年毛泽东去世时，这位中国共产党创始人的遗体被安放在一口以最上乘东海石英制成的透明棺木中。

paragraphs.en: Rural China was still deeply poor; decades of collectivized farming had kept incomes low. After Deng Xiaoping took power and loosened some of the government’s rigid economic controls, people suddenly had the freedom to build small, scrappy businesses known as township and village enterprises. Their success blindsided even top party leaders. “Every year, township and village industries achieved 20 percent growth,” Deng later reflected to a group of journalists. “This was not something I had thought about. Nor had the other comrades. This surprised us.”
paragraphs.zh: 当时的乡村中国仍然非常贫困；几十年集体化农业使得收入持续偏低。邓小平执政后放松了部分僵化的国家经济管制，人们突然有机会创建以小作坊起家的“脏活儿”企业，即乡镇企业。连党的高级领导人都被其成功震惊了。“每年，乡镇和村办工业都实现了20%的增长，”邓后来对一群记者这样回顾道。“这不是我事先想过的，其他同志也没想过。这让我们很惊讶。”

paragraphs.en: Villages looked for niches they could fill in the global market. The town of Xuchang, for instance, capitalized on its legacy of making hairpieces for opera performers—and on the willingness of rural women to sell their black ponytails—and turned itself into a hub for wigs. Zhuangzhai became the largest supplier of caskets to Japan, in part through its proximity to groves of paulownia, a lightweight, slow-burning wood favored in Japanese cremation ceremonies. The town of Qiaotou became the world’s button-making capital after three brothers found a handful of discarded buttons in a gutter and decided to resell them, or so the story goes.
paragraphs.zh: 各村镇开始寻找自己能在全球市场占据的细分领域。比如，许昌镇凭借其长期为戏曲演员制作假发的传统经验，以及农村妇女乐于出售黑色马尾辫的做法，最终转型为假发产业中心。庄寨则在成为日本棺木最大供应地方面拔得头筹，部分原因在于其靠近大量梧桐树林；梧桐木轻巧且燃烧较慢，是日本火化仪式中偏好的木材。桥头镇在一个关于三兄弟在排水沟里捡到一把丢弃扣子、并决定转手出售的传闻推动下，后来成长为世界按钮制造之都。

paragraphs.en: Donghai already had plenty of quartz and skilled labor, as well as entrepreneurs who were willing to experiment. Wu Qingfeng, a former editor at the Crystal Museum who now leads boot camps for wannabe crystal entrepreneurs, says that in the late 1980s, artisans learned to modify washing machine motors so they could polish crystal necklaces, previously a manual job. When there wasn’t enough raw crystal to keep up with demand, manufacturers resorted to glass from beer bottles to make beads. People in Donghai told us they recall the shortage becoming so dire at one point that restaurants and bars ran out of beer.
paragraphs.zh: 东海本身本就拥有丰富的石英和熟练劳动力，再加上乐于试验的创业者。前任“水晶博物馆”编辑、如今又在为有志于水晶创业者开设训练营的吴青峰表示，20世纪80年代末，工匠们学会了改装洗衣机电机，用于抛光水晶项链——这在此之前一直是手工完成的。 当时原石供应不足以支撑不断上升的需求时，厂商转而用啤酒瓶玻璃冒险制作珠子。东海的居民告诉我们，短缺曾经严重到某个阶段，餐馆和酒吧都已经把啤酒卖完了。

paragraphs.en: Around the same time, illegal mining was spiraling out of control. All the digging was causing roads to collapse and houses to sink, sometimes leading to injuries and deaths, according to Chinese media. In late 2001, Donghai County authorities warned of an impending crackdown on unauthorized mining. With the domestic crystal supply tightening up, local entrepreneurs were increasingly traveling all over the world to find new sources of raw material. As one executive from a crystal industry group told a newspaper, “Wherever there are raw stones, there are people from Donghai.”
paragraphs.zh: 与此同时，非法采矿也在失控地失序扩张。中国媒体报道称，过度挖掘导致道路塌陷和房屋下沉，进而引发了伤亡。2001年末，东海县当局警告称将对无证采矿进行严厉打击。随着国内水晶供应日益收紧，当地创业者越来越频繁地走向世界各地寻找新的原料来源。正如一位水晶行业协会高管对一家报纸所言，“哪里有原石，哪里就有来自东海的人。”

paragraphs.en: Venturing to far-flung locales was seen not as daring but simply as the default mode of doing business, says Kyle Chan, a fellow at the Brookings Institution who specializes in Chinese industrial policy. In China, there’s “this idea, almost like overconfidence, that you can just go wherever in the world and outwork and outmaneuver whoever,” Chan says. People tend not to “see the cultural barriers as, like, real barriers.”
paragraphs.zh: 布鲁金斯学会的中国工业政策研究员陈凯乐（Kyle Chan）表示，到遥远地区“跑业务”在当地人看来并不算胆大冒险，而更像一种默认做法。在中国，人们有一种“几乎接近自负”的观念：可以到世界任何地方去，并通过更强的执行力与更快的应对把竞争对手压过。他说，人们往往并不把文化差异当作“真正的障碍”。

paragraphs.en: Wu Qingfeng says that Donghai traders were astonished by the riches to be found abroad. They learned about enormous deposits in Africa, he says, after people in a neighboring province traveled there to participate in a humanitarian project. Some countries had so much quartz that they were paving roads with it. In Donghai, the crystal deposits are scattered, Wu says, “but when you go to Madagascar, Zambia, the Congo, and other countries, you find that the local rose quartz is like coal—an entire mountain is rose quartz.”
paragraphs.zh: 武清锋表示，东海的贸易商们被国外的财富震惊了。他说，他们是在邻省有人前往非洲参加一项人道主义项目后才得知那里有巨大的矿藏的。某些国家的石英多得可以用来铺路。在东海，水晶矿脉是散布的，武清锋说，“但你去马达加斯加、赞比亚、刚果等国家会发现，当地的粉晶就像煤一样——一整座山都是粉晶。”

paragraphs.en: Liu, the owner of Big Purple Crystal, says he began traveling abroad to look for amethyst about a decade ago. His first stop was Brazil. “I got a cheap plane ticket and brought along a translator,” he says. “The next day, I bought my first shipping container—about 20 tons of goods.” But Liu struggled to make money, so he searched for opportunities elsewhere. At the Tucson Gem and Mineral Show in Arizona, a sprawling annual gathering, he came across impressive amethyst pieces from Uruguay, and he decided to go there.
paragraphs.zh: Big Purple Crystal 的老板刘说，他大约十年前开始出国寻找紫水晶。第一站是巴西。“我买了张便宜机票，还带了个翻译，”他讲述道，“第二天，我就买下了我的第一个集装箱，大约20吨货。”但刘的赚钱之路并不顺利，于是他开始在别处寻找机会。在亚利桑那州的图森宝石和矿物展这一场规模庞大的年度展会上，他看到了来自乌拉圭的令人印象深刻的紫水晶制品，于是决定去那里。

paragraphs.en: One wall of the Big Purple Crystal warehouse is lined with photos of Liu posing with Uruguayan diplomats and politicians, including the former president. His biggest gripe about working in the country, he says, is the lack of Chinese food. Liu’s daughter, who is in art school, now also makes trips to Uruguay for the family business. When the miners there dig up a particularly good piece, she snaps a photo and sends it to one of several group chats. “If someone wants it, it sells immediately,” Liu says. “She can flip it right away.”
paragraphs.zh: Big Purple Crystal 仓库的一面墙上，贴满了刘与乌拉圭外交官和政客合影的照片，其中包括前总统。刘说他在这个国家工作最大的抱怨是缺少中餐。如今，正在上艺术院校的刘的女儿也会前往乌拉圭处理家族生意。当那里的矿工挖出一块特别好的矿石时，她会拍张照并发到几个群聊里。“如果有人想要，就会马上卖掉，”刘说，“她可以立刻把它转手。”

paragraphs.en: It’s 7 am, and the raw crystal flea market in Donghai’s Xingxi village is bustling. Merchants sit in camping chairs and bicker beside tarps piled with stones. Artisans pick up unassuming rocks and shine powerful flashlights through them. A livestreamer holds a big bag of pink crystal bracelets with one hand and shouts into a tiny phone he clutches with the other. “200 units at just 9.9!” he barks. An elderly villager watching nearby does some quick math under his breath—almost $300 for just five minutes of broadcasting. There’s a hint of awe in his voice; maybe a touch of envy too.
paragraphs.zh: 现在是早上7点，东海星溪村的原石跳蚤市场已经十分热闹。商贩们坐在露营椅上，在堆满石头的篷布旁争价。工匠捡起一些看似普通的石头，用强力手电筒照射其中。一个主播一只手抓着一大袋粉晶手链，另一只手紧握着小手机喊道：“200件只要9.9！”一位旁边的老村民在心里快算了一下——仅仅五分钟直播就快有300美元了。他的语气里带着一丝惊讶，也可能有点羡慕。

paragraphs.en: The livestreamer, Zhao Zhonggang, is the Communist Party secretary of Xingxi, a village of some 4,000 people where he says roughly 70 percent of residents work in the crystal trade. Online, where he’s known as the Crystal Secretary, Zhao posts videos of his daily life—touring the flea market to help vendors sell, chatting with elders, and playing basketball with local kids. One of the biggest concerns for crystal buyers is the risk of fakes, and few things are more reassuring than an official title. Zhao’s credibility carries so much weight that fans have driven across the country just to meet him in person, according to local media reports.
paragraphs.zh: 这位主播赵中华岗是兴溪村的书记。这个村子大约有4,000人，他说约70%的村民都在从事水晶生意。在线上，他以“水晶书记”的身份出现，发布自己日常生活的视频——逛跳蚤市场帮摊贩带货、和老人聊天、和本地孩子打篮球。对水晶买家而言，最大担忧之一是买到假货，而官方职务比许多东西都更能带来保障。赵的信誉非常重，地方媒体报道称，粉丝们甚至跨省驱车来见他本人。

paragraphs.en: Zhao is a particularly Chinese kind of politician, the product of a system that evaluates officials based on how their districts perform economically. In this world, the boundary between governing and doing business blurs. Zhao’s office isn’t in some stately municipal building—it’s down the hall from a workshop where women string crystal bracelets by hand. Right outside the door is a stockpile of crystal skulls, zodiac animals, and even penises. (Overseas customers prefer products like this that are “a bit strange-looking,” one of Zhao’s employees explains. “There’s simply no market for them in China.”)
paragraphs.zh: 赵特别像一个“中国式官员”，是一个以地方经济表现来考核干部的体制下的产物。在这样的环境里，治理与经商之间的边界会变得模糊。赵的办公室不在庄重宏大的市政楼里，而是就在一个车间走廊尽头，那里的女性正在手工穿制水晶手链。门外正好堆着一批水晶骷髅、生肖摆件，甚至还有阴茎造型产品。（赵的一名员工解释说，海外客户更喜欢这类“看起来有点怪”的商品。“在中国根本没有市场。”）

paragraphs.en: Zhao’s journey began in 2012, when he became an assistant for a nearby village committee. He noticed that piles of crystal fragments from local workshops were being wasted. Later, under his leadership, villagers began repurposing them for higher-end products. They arranged the bits into mosaic lampshades and sewed them into “healing” pillows and mattresses, contributing to millions of dollars in domestic and international sales.
paragraphs.zh: 赵的经历始于2012年，当时他成为了一个附近村委会的助理。他发现当地作坊里产生的水晶碎片长期被浪费掉。后来，在他的带领下，村民开始把这些碎片再利用，做出更高端的产品。他们把碎片拼成拼花灯罩，并将其缝进“疗愈枕”和“疗愈床垫”，从而推动了数百万美元的国内外销售额。

paragraphs.en: Zhao was transferred to Xingxi in 2019. As pandemic lockdowns pushed commerce online, he launched an account on Douyin, the Chinese version of TikTok, and racked up followers, helping Xingxi earn the nickname “24/7 Livestream Village.” At first, people were broadcasting mostly to domestic shoppers. But the following year, the clattering sound of Donghai crystals began radiating from smartphones across the US and UK.
paragraphs.zh: 赵于2019年被调到兴溪。随着疫情封控让商业活动更多转向线上，他在中国版TikTok——抖音上开通了账号，并迅速积累粉丝，帮助兴溪获得了“24/7直播村”的绰号。起初，人们主要向国内买家直播。可下一年，东海水晶清脆的碰撞声就从智能手机里辐射到了美国和英国。

paragraphs.en: Under the glow of ring lights, TikTok livestreamers plunged metal scoops into massive buckets, pouring amethyst bracelets, chunks of lapis, and towers of clear quartz into baskets and plastic bags. Above the sparkling piles, signs beckoned viewers to spend between $2.99 and $9.99 for their own “lucky scoop.” By the end of 2022, crystals had become one of the best-selling products on TikTok Shop globally.
paragraphs.zh: 在环形补光灯的光晕下，抖音直播主把金属铲子插入巨大的桶中，将紫水晶手链、拉长石块和晶莹剔透的石英塔状摆件倒进篮子和塑料袋。闪闪发光的货堆上方，标牌引导观众花2.99美元到9.99美元购买自己的“幸运一勺”。到2022年底，水晶已成为TikTok Shop在全球最畅销的商品之一。

paragraphs.en: “There was explosive growth,” Liu recalls. “The entire crystal industry was shipping to the US.” Local residents opened livestreaming “supermarkets,” where hundreds of streamers could broadcast at once. The government simplified licensing and compliance requirements and partnered with a state bank to launch a foreign-currency settlement program, lowering the cost of selling directly to buyers abroad.
paragraphs.zh: “增长非常惊人，”刘回忆道。“整个水晶行业都在向美国发货。”当地居民开设了直播“超市”，让数百位主播可以同时开播。政府简化了许可和合规流程，并与一家国有银行合作推出了外币结算计划，降低了直接向海外买家销售的成本。

paragraphs.en: Together, these initiatives helped Donghai collapse the old crystal supply chain. Instead of passing through layers of wholesalers and retail stores, goods could now flow straight from the crystal capital to foreign consumers, cutting out the middlemen and lowering prices. “Donghai people were extremely good at the internet,” says Liu. At the market’s peak, he says, Big Purple Crystal could sell an entire shipping container of amethyst in one or two hours.
paragraphs.zh: 这些举措共同推动了东海旧有水晶供应链的崩解。过去货物要层层经过批发商和零售店，如今则可以从“水晶中心”直接流向海外消费者，省去中间环节并降低价格。刘说：“东海人对互联网非常在行。”在市场高峰期，他表示，Big Purple Crystal可以在一到两个小时内售出一整集装箱的紫水晶。

paragraphs.en: Wu Qingfeng, the boot camp instructor, says this golden period lasted a year or two. Some companies recruited live-streamers who had no professional experience and could speak only a little English. They would go on camera and say all kinds of nonsense, Wu recalls, and still potentially make thousands of dollars a day. But then customers started to catch on that different sellers were broadcasting from the same building, Wu says: “Everyone understood: It turned out you were all one Chinese supermarket.”
paragraphs.zh: 直播训练营教练吴清风说，这个黄金期只持续了一两年。一些公司招募了没有专业经验、只会说一点英语的主播。吴回忆说，他们上镜后会胡说八道，却仍有机会每天赚几千美元。但后来顾客开始看穿，发现不同的卖家其实都在同一栋楼里直播，吴说：“大家都明白了：原来你们全是一个中国超市。”

paragraphs.en: Customers started searching for the best deals, and a brutal price war broke out in Donghai. “This cross-border supermarket business became mutually malicious competition,” says Wu. TikTok also started cracking down on scammier, low-quality crystal content, and even the lucky scoop format was eventually banned. By the summer of 2023, he says, many small- and midsize crystal businesses started to get wiped out.
paragraphs.zh: 顾客开始搜罗最划算的商品，东海随即爆发了一场残酷的价格战。“这场跨境超市生意演变成了互相恶意的竞争，”吴说。抖音（TikTok）也开始严查更具诈骗色彩、质量更差的“水晶内容”，甚至连“lucky scoop”这种内容形式最终也被禁止。到2023年夏天，他说，许多小型和中型的水晶商家开始被清退。

paragraphs.en: The downturn has dragged on. But in Donghai, another surge always feels just around the corner. Inside one market, a sign advertised “crystal express loans” for local merchants, with flexible repayment terms designed to keep cash flowing. At a seminar for cross-border sellers, a representative from China Mobile, the state-owned telecom, advertised a service that lets companies bypass China’s so-called Great Firewall to set up shops on international platforms like TikTok and Temu. It was pitched as a government-approved substitute for VPNs, which are tightly restricted in China.
paragraphs.zh: 这场下行压力一直拖着不去。但在东海，另一波反弹总像就在拐角处。某个集市里，一块招牌为本地商户广告“水晶快贷”，并提供灵活还款条款以维持资金流转。在一次跨境卖家研讨会上，中国移动的一位代表介绍了一项服务：让企业绕过中国所谓的“防火长城”，在 TikTok 和 Temu 等国际平台开设店铺。这被称作是政府认证的 VPN 替代方案，而这些方式在中国受到严格限制。

paragraphs.en: In the US, the core tactics Donghai has used—subsidizing local businesses, building communal infrastructure, and aggressively pursuing global market share—are viewed with deep suspicion. Rather than savvy development tools, US lawmakers and pundits have framed them as unfair practices that undermine “market-oriented competition” and put American companies at a disadvantage. This rhetoric has fueled a popular narrative about China’s economic growth that is incomplete, reduced to little more than nefarious state interference.
paragraphs.zh: 在美国，东海采用的核心做法——补贴本地企业、建设公共基础设施、并激进地争夺全球市场份额——被视为充满深深的疑虑。美国立法者和评论员并不把这些当作成熟的产业发展手段，而是把它们塑造成破坏“市场化竞争”、并使美国公司处于不利地位的不公平做法。此种叙事助长了美国社会对中国经济增长的一种流行说法：它是不完整的、被简化为肮脏国家干预的故事。

paragraphs.en: But in Donghai, people told us that success often comes through shameless, cutthroat rivalry. Rather than being stigmatized, imitation is seen as practical. When one person invents a new product that does well (say, crystals shaped like fairy wings or carved to look like Pokémon), others immediately tweak it and start selling their own versions. “This is something much deeper about China and the Chinese market, this whole very-fast-follower dynamic,” says Chan, the Brookings fellow. Robert Wu, CEO of the China-focused market research firm BigOne Lab, contrasts that with the American view: You “want to do something unique, and once someone else is already doing it, you tend not to follow.”
paragraphs.zh: 但在东海，人们告诉我们，成功往往来自于无所顾忌、又充满厮杀意味的竞争。模仿并不被当成污点，而被看作务实之举。当一个人发明出畅销新产品（比如做成仙女翅膀形状的水晶，或刻成宝可梦样式）后，其他人会立刻改一改并推出自己的版本。“这背后有更深层的东西，关于中国和中国市场，整个这种超快跟随的动态，”布鲁金斯学者 Chan 说。专注于中国市场的研究机构 BigOne Lab 的首席执行官吴铮（Robert Wu）将其与美国观念做了对比：在美国，你“想做一件独特的事，一旦别人已经在做，你通常就不会跟随。”

paragraphs.en: Just across the street from Liu’s warehouse sits International Crystal Jewelry City Plaza, a megamall packed with some 7,000 businesses hawking everything from stretchy bracelet cord to loose emeralds. A flea market is happening out front today, and Wu is preparing 20 or so of his boot camp students for the impending chaos.
paragraphs.zh: 在刘的仓库隔街不远处，是国际水晶珠宝城广场，这座大型购物中心里挤满了大约7,000家店铺，出售从弹性手链绳到松散翡翠（翠绿色）都无所不有。今天，广场门口正举办集市，吴正在为即将到来的乱象准备大约20名左右的直播训练营学员。

paragraphs.en: “When buying at the market, don’t ask the vendor if it’s real or fake—don’t,” Wu says. “They’ll think you can’t even tell, so why wouldn’t they scam you?”
paragraphs.zh: 吴说：“在集市买东西时，不要问摊主这是不是正品——别问。要是你这么问，他们会觉得你连分辨都不会，所以为什么不来坑你呢？”

paragraphs.en: Another tip: Don’t be too eager to stockpile inventory, because prices fluctuate. White quartz, he explains, now sells for just 10 to 20 percent of its most recent high. “What are you supposed to do with that inventory if you bought it at the higher price?” Wu asks. “How much ‘mystical energy’ would you have to add to it before you could even sell it?” He tells the students he recently heard about the owner of a crystal factory who bought a huge order of white quartz before prices dropped and ended up jumping off a building.
paragraphs.zh: 另一个建议是：别太急着囤货，因为价格会波动。他解释说，白水晶如今只卖到最近最高价的10%到20%。“你如果是按更高的价格买下去的，这批库存你打算怎么办？”吴问道。“你要往上面加多少‘神秘能量’，才勉强能卖出去？”他还告诉学员们，最近他听说有一家水晶厂的老板在价格下跌前大批进货白水晶，最后竟跳楼了。

paragraphs.en: A few blocks away, a different kind of glittering industry is on the rise in Donghai: elaborate, gem-encrusted press-on nails. News reports tout press-ons as the region’s next signature export, part of a so-called “fingertip economy” built around small, handmade goods designed for livestreaming and fast international shipping. One wholesaler estimates that Donghai already produces roughly 70 percent of China’s press-on nails, at a scale of around 400,000 sets a day.
paragraphs.zh: 在离这里不远的几条街之外，东海正在兴起另一种闪闪发光的产业：精致的、镶嵌宝石的贴甲片。新闻报道把贴甲片推为该地区的下一张名片式出口产品，是所谓“指尖经济”的一部分——这种经济围绕小型手工制品、直播带货以及快速国际邮寄展开。一位批发商估计，东海已经生产了中国大约70%的贴甲片，日均产量在40万套左右。

paragraphs.en: At one nail shop, the walls are lined with metal racks of clear plastic packages, each containing a set of 10 nails. There are hundreds of options, and they all cost less than $2. A hedonistic urge takes over, and we begin grabbing things—shimmery pale pink nails painted with swirling koi fish, a long green set with 3D lotus flowers glued on each thumb. In the corner of the store is a workstation splattered with nail polish and glue, hinting at the painstaking human labor behind the finished products.
paragraphs.zh: 在一家美甲店里，墙上排列着金属架，架子上放着透明塑料包装盒，每个盒子里是一套10只指甲。选择有成百上千种，全部都低于2美元。此时我们很容易被一种挥霍式冲动驱使，开始不停地捡东西——闪亮的浅粉色指甲上画着盘旋的锦鲤，或者是一套长绿色指甲，每根拇指上都粘着3D的莲花图案。店里角落的工作台上溅满了指甲油和胶水，隐约透露出这些成品背后那种细致而耗费人力的制作过程。

paragraphs.en: The shop’s owner, a woman in her forties with a young daughter, tells us she brings in $200,000 to $300,000 annually, income she credits largely to livestreaming. Business is especially strong in niche markets like Japan and South Korea, where she says competition is thinner and margins are higher. As the shop owner chats with us and sorts through paperwork, a young female livestreamer stands in front of a rack, speaking into her phone in Japanese. She holds up a set of nails, tilting them back and forth, as a ribbon of light flashes across their surface. The color is like deep blue quartz—saturated and luminous.
paragraphs.zh: 店主是一位四十多岁的女士，带着一个小女儿。她告诉我们，她每年的营业额在20万到30万美元之间，而她把这主要归功于直播带货。她说，日本和韩国这类细分市场的生意特别好，因为那里竞争没那么激烈、利润更高。当店主一边和我们聊天一边整理文件时，一位年轻的女性直播员站在货架前，用日语对着手机讲话。她拿起一盒指甲，在空中左右轻轻一摇，一束光线便掠过指甲表面。那种颜色像深蓝色的晶体，浓烈且有光泽。

paragraphs.en: What Say You?Let us know what you think about this article in the comments below. Alternatively, you can submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh: 你怎么看？欢迎在下方评论区告诉我们你对这篇文章的看法。你也可以通过 [email protected] 向编辑部来信。
